elements.sent-2411-001: Regulation of gene expression is the consequence of interactions between the promoter of the gene and the transcription factors (TFs).
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we explore the features of a genetic network where the TFs (activators and repressors) bind the promoter in a competitive way.
elements.sent-2411-003: We develop an analytical theory that offers detailed reaction kinetics of the competitive activator-repressor system which could be the powerful tools for extensive study and analysis of the genetic circuit in future research.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, the theoretical approach helps us to find a most probable set of parameter values which was unavailable in experiments.
elements.sent-2411-005: We study the noisy behaviour of the circuit and compare the profile with the network where the activator and repressor bind the promoter non-competitively.
elements.sent-2411-006: We further notice that, due to the effect of transcriptional reinitiation in the presence of the activator and repressor molecules, there exits some anomalous characteristic features in the mean expressions and noise profiles.
elements.sent-2411-007: We find that, in presence of the reinitiation the noise in transcriptional level remains low while it is higher in translational level than the noise when the reinitiation is absent.
elements.sent-2411-008: In addition, it is possible to reduce the noise further below the Poissonian level in competitive circuit than the non-competitive one with the help of some noise reducing parameters.
elements.sent-2411-009: For the last two decades, by employing several experiments, it has been widely verified and accepted that the gene expression regulation is a stochastic process \cite{key-1,key-2,key-3,key-4,key-5,key-6,key-7,key-8,key-9,key-10,key-11 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In parallel with the experiments, many theoretical approaches, especially analytical analyses and numerical simulations have built up in support \cite{key-12,key-13,key-14,key-15,key-16,key-17,key-18,key-19,key-20,key-21,key-22 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Regulation of gene expression (GE) is essential for all organisms to develop their ability to respond to environmental changes.
elements.sent-2411-012: It involves several complex stochastic mechanisms such as transcription, preinitiation, reinitiation, translation and degradation, etc. \cite{key-23,key-24 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Transcription is the initial step through which the biological information is being transferred from the genome to the proteome \cite{key-25 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The regulation of transcription \cite{key-18,key-19 occurs whenever some regulatory proteins called transcription factors (TFs) interact with the promoter of a gene.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Based on functionality, TFs are classified as activators and repressors in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes \cite{key-25,key-26,key-27 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Repressors inhibit the gene transcription by binding to specific DNA sequences.
elements.sent-2411-017: Tryptophan and Lac are some widely known repressors for prokaryotic systems.
elements.sent-2411-018: On the other hand, activators allow RNA-Polymerase (RNAP) to bind with the promoter and to initiate the transcription resulting in synthesizing mRNA.
elements.sent-2411-019: The molecular concentrations of these activators and/or repressors can be varied with the help of some inducer molecules, such as doxycycline (dox), galactose (GAL), tetracycline (Tc) and anhydrotetracycline (aTc), etc. \cite{key-3,key-10,key-11 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: An eukaryotic system is much more complex and possesses a compact chromatin structure than prokaryotes.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the eukaryotic system, a basic example of transcriptional regulation by TFs is a two-state telegraphic model \cite{key-14,key-15,key-17 , where a gene can be either in any of the two states: active (ON) or inactive (OFF) depending on whether TFs are bound to the gene or not.}
elements.sent-2411-022: An active gene transcribes through the production of messenger-RNA (mRNA) in a pulsatile fashion during short intense periods known as transcriptional bursts, followed by a longer period of inactivity.
elements.sent-2411-023: This burst mechanism is the source of cellular heterogeneity and stochastic noise \cite{key-7,key-15,key-16 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Another crucial mechanism of transcription known as reinitiation \cite{key-3,key-28 is the cause of heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-025: This fact has been proved by experiments \cite{key-3,key-6,key-9,key-29,key-30,key-31 and recent theories \cite{key-32,key-33,key-34 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Although most of the theories describe GE models using numerical simulations, discrete stochastic models like the Gillespie algorithm \cite{key-35 and continuous stochastic models involving the chemical Langevin equation (CLE) \cite{key-36 .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The use of the Gillespie algorithm and CLE are limited as they require details of the biochemical reaction kinetics and rate parameters which are often unavailable in the performed biological experiments.
elements.sent-2411-028: Due to these limitations, many biological systems remain unmodeled or modelled without stochastic simulations.
elements.sent-2411-029: One such experiment, carried out by Rossi et al. \cite{key-37 was not modelled.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The authors used a dox-controlled synthetic transcription unit driven by activator only, repressor only, or both in an overlapping promoter region.
elements.sent-2411-031: They proposed that the dose-response curve follows the Hill function and the Hill coefficients from the dose-response curves are observed as: 1.6 in the presence of the activator only, 1.8 in the presence of the repressor only and 3.2 when both the activator and repressor operating together exclusively.
elements.sent-2411-032: Rossi et al. suggested that either addition or multiplication of the Hill coefficients 1.6 and 1.8 gives 3.4 and 2.88 ($\approx$2.9) respectively, which differs from what they found to be 3.2.
elements.sent-2411-033: Almost a decade later, Yang and Ko \cite{key-38 proposed a stochastic Markov chain model (MCM) with a three-state activator-repressor system to explain the deviation of observed Hill coefficients from the expected values.}

elements.sent-2411-035: It is thus desirable to explain the model with a proper theory supported by analytical and/or simulation methods.

elements.sent-2411-211: Fano factor and Noise strength : The Fano factor is the measure of deviations of noise from the Poissonian behavior and is defined as \cite{key-42,key-43 ,.}
elements.sent-2411-212: \begin{equation}Fano\thinspace factor=\frac{variance}{mean}=\frac{(standard\thinspace deviation)^{2}}{mean}\end{equation} So, for a given mean, smaller the Fano factor implies smaller variance and thus less noise.
elements.sent-2411-213: Therefore, the Fano factor gives a measure of noise strength which is defined (mathematically) as \cite{key-2 , \begin{equation}noise\thinspace strength=\frac{variance}{mean}=\frac{(standard\thinspace deviation)^{2}}{mean}\end{equation} So, the Fano factor and noise strength are synonymous.}

elements.sent-2411-242: Over the years, it has been experimentally verified and accepted that the gene expression regulation is an inherent stochastic process \cite{elowitz2002stochastic,ozbudak2002regulation,blake2003noise,raser2005noise,golding2005real,blake2006phenotypic,raj2006stochastic,suter2011mammalian,bartman2019transcriptional,thattai2004stochastic,biggar2001cell .}
elements.sent-2411-243: In parallel with the experiments, many theoretical approaches, analytical and numerical simulations, have been proposed in support \cite{paulson2005modelling,sanchez2008transcriptional,shahrezaei2008analytical,karmakar2004graded,karmakar2010conversion,kumar2014exact,bintu2005transcriptional,bintu2005transcriptionalapp,kuhlman2007combinatorial,vilar2003dna,vilar2005dna .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Regulation of gene expression (GE) is essential for all organisms to develop their ability to respond to the environmental changes.
elements.sent-2411-245: It involves several complex stochastic mechanisms such as transcription, pre-initiation, reinitiation, translation, and degradation, etc. \cite{kaern2005stochasticity,raj2008nature .}
elements.sent-2411-246: Transcription is the initial step through which the biological information is being transferred from the genome to the proteome \cite{alberts2002helper .}
elements.sent-2411-247: The regulation of transcription \cite{bintu2005transcriptional,bintu2005transcriptionalapp occurs whenever some regulatory proteins called transcription factors (TFs) interact with the promoter of a gene.}
elements.sent-2411-248: Based on functionality, TFs are classified as activators and repressors in both prokaryotes and eukaryotes \cite{alberts2002helper,ptashne2005regulation,struhl1999fundamentally .}
elements.sent-2411-249: On the other hand, activators allow RNA-Polymerase (RNAP) to bind to the promoter and initiate the transcription, resulting in synthesizing mRNA.
elements.sent-2411-250: The molecular concentrations of these activators and/or repressors can be varied with the help of some inducer molecules, such as doxycycline (dox), galactose (GAL), tetracycline (Tc) and anhydrotetracycline (aTc), etc. \cite{blake2003noise,thattai2004stochastic,biggar2001cell .}
elements.sent-2411-251: In the eukaryotic system, a basic example of transcriptional regulation by TFs is a two-state telegraphic model \cite{shahrezaei2008analytical,karmakar2004graded,kumar2014exact , where a gene can be active (ON) or inactive (OFF) depending on whether TFs are bound to the gene or not, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-252: This burst mechanism is the source of cellular heterogeneity and stochastic noise \cite{raj2006stochastic,karmakar2004graded,karmakar2010conversion .}
elements.sent-2411-253: Another crucial mechanism of transcription known as reinitiation \cite{blake2003noise,liu2014reinitiation is the cause of heterogeneity.}
elements.sent-2411-254: This fact has been proven by experiments \cite{blake2003noise,blake2006phenotypic,bartman2019transcriptional,shao2017paused,yudkovsky2000transcription as well as by recent theories \cite{cao2020stochastic,karmakar2020control,karmakar2021effect .}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Most of the theories describe GE models using numerical simulations, discrete stochastic models like the Gillespie algorithm \cite{gillespie1977exact and continuous stochastic models involving the chemical Langevin equation (CLE) \cite{gardiner2009handbook .}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Due to these limitations, many biological systems remain unmodeled or modeled without stochastic simulations.

elements.sent-2411-438: Transcription factor: Transcription factors (TFs) are proteins that have DNA binding domains with the ability to bind to the specific sequences of DNA (called promoter).
elements.sent-2411-439: They control the rate of transcription.
elements.sent-2411-440: If they enhance transcription they are called activators and repressors if they inhibit transcription.
elements.sent-2411-441: Fano factor and Noise strength: The Fano factor is the measure of deviations of noise from the Poissonian behavior and is defined as \cite{das2022stochastic,braichenko2021distinguishing , \begin{equation}Fano\,factor\,(mRNA)=\frac{variance\thinspace of\,protein}{mean\,mRNA}=\frac{(standard\,deviation)^{2}}{mean}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-442: So, for a given mean, smaller the Fano factor implies smaller variance and thus less noise.
elements.sent-2411-443: Therefore, the Fano factor gives a measure of noise strength which is defined (mathematically) as \cite{blake2003noise , \begin{equation}noise\ strength=\frac{variance\thinspace of\,protein}{mean\,mRNA}=\frac{(standard\,deviation)^{2}}{mean}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-444: So, the Fano factor and noise strength are synonymous.
elements.sent-2411-445: Transcriptional efficiency : Transcriptional efficiency is the ratio of instantaneous transcription to the maximum transcription \cite{blake2003noise .}
